summary: Tractate Yoma daf 78a covers: Temple river bubbles up from beneath the threshold and becomes mighty; Crossing water on Yom Kippur: does it constitute prohibited bathing?; Sick person wearing shoes on YK; wooden shoes; danger exceptions; Rain water, river crossing, measuring bathing by amount; Unresolved question about water crossing and the shoe prohibition.

display.whats: Temple river bubbles up from beneath the threshold and becomes mighty
display.hint: The aggadic description of the future Temple river: it begins as a trickle from beneath the Temple threshold and grows into a mighty river that cannot be crossed on foot.

learning.summary: The aggadic description of the future Temple river: it begins as a trickle from beneath the Temple threshold and grows into a mighty river that cannot be crossed on foot.
learning.ahaMoment: The Temple river aggadah is placed here because this sugya discusses water and crossing - the Talmud's associative logic connects mundane halacha to eschatological vision.
learning.memoryAnchor: Temple river aggadah: begins as a trickle, ends as a torrent - placed here because of the water discussion.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about temple river bubbles up from beneath the threshold and becom?

argumentFlow.label: The growing spring becomes a river of purification
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The future spring strengthens past the Temple threshold until it reaches the opening of David's house, where it becomes a flowing river in which zavim, zavot, menstruating women, and women after childbirth bathe to purify, as Zechariah foretells a fountain opened for purification.

argumentFlow.label: Rejecting Rav Yosef's neck-deep mikveh requirement
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Yosef infers from the verse that a menstruating woman must immerse in water deep enough to sit up to her neck. The Gemara rules against him: depth is irrelevant, and a ritual bath is valid as long as the water can cover an entire adult body.

theological: The Temple river aggadah is placed here because this sugya discusses water and c
narrative: Temple river bubbles up from beneath the threshold and becom

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of Temple river context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners take Rav Yosef's neck-deep image as the operative law for mikveh dimensions.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara explicitly does not follow Rav Yosef. The verse's imagery is not the standard; the accepted measure is simply that the water suffice to cover a person's whole body.

visualizableElements.name: Temple river bubbles up from beneath the threshold

quizSeeds.q: Why does the Gemara reject Rav Yosef's claim that a mikveh must be deep enough to sit neck-high?
quizSeeds.a: Because the halachic requirement is volume, not depth. A ritual bath is valid as long as it can cover an entire adult body at once; how deep one can sit in it is not the criterion.

sugyot.finalRuling: The aggadic description of the future Temple river: it begins as a trickle from beneath the Temple threshold and grows into a mighty river that cannot

display.whats: Crossing water on Yom Kippur: does it constitute prohibited bathing?
display.hint: The Gemara asks whether crossing a river or stream on Yom Kippur constitutes the prohibited bathing. The answer depends on intent and manner of crossing.

learning.summary: The Gemara asks whether crossing a river or stream on Yom Kippur constitutes the prohibited bathing. The answer depends on intent and manner of crossing.
learning.ahaMoment: Intent matters for bathing: crossing water to get somewhere is different from immersing for pleasure. The prohibition targets the pleasure, not the water.
learning.memoryAnchor: Crossing water on YK: intent and manner determine whether it is bathing.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about crossing water on yom kippur: does it constitute prohibited ?

argumentFlow.label: Crossing water in shoes on Shabbat
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Crossing water is straightforward on Yom Kippur because no shoes are worn, but on Shabbat shoes raise a concern: a shoe might slip off in the water and one would pick it up, violating carrying. Nechemya reports seeing Rabbi Ami and Rabbi Asi cross a pool on Shabbat while still wearing their shoes.

argumentFlow.label: Tight shoes versus loose sandals
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Shoes can be tied tightly, so the concern is lighter, but sandals fit loosely and might come off in the water. Rav Richumi saw Ravina cross a river in sandals, yet Rav Ashi rules that one should not cross in sandals ab initio, lest one fall off and he carry it on Shabbat.

argumentFlow.label: Ravina's absence from the lecture
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Exilarch lectured in Hagronya and Ravina did not attend. The next day Rafram, to dispel any resentment, asked Ravina why he was absent, and Ravina explained that his foot hurt, specifically the back of the foot, which made wearing closed shoes difficult.

argumentFlow.label: Ravina applies Rav Ashi's sandal ruling to himself
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rafram suggested heel-exposing sandals, but Ravina noted he would have had to cross a pool. When Rafram said to cross in the sandals, Ravina invoked Rav Ashi's ruling that one should not cross water in sandals ab initio, showing his absence reflected halachic caution, not disrespect.

theological: Intent matters for bathing: crossing water to get somewhere is different from im
narrative: Crossing water on Yom Kippur: does it constitute prohibited

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of crossing water context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners assume the water-crossing rules are the same on Yom Kippur and Shabbat.
misconceptions.correction: The two days raise different concerns. On Yom Kippur no shoes are worn, so crossing is simple; on Shabbat the live issue is footwear slipping off and being carried, which is why the discussion centers on shoes versus sandals.

visualizableElements.name: Crossing water on Yom Kippur: does it constitute p

quizSeeds.q: Why is crossing water in sandals on Shabbat more problematic than crossing in shoes?
quizSeeds.a: Shoes can be tied on tightly, but sandals fit loosely and may slip off in the water. If a sandal comes off and one picks it up, he violates the Shabbat prohibition of carrying, so Rav Ashi forbids crossing in sandals ab initio.

quizSeeds.q: How does Ravina's explanation for missing the lecture turn on a point of law?
quizSeeds.a: Ravina's foot pain ruled out closed shoes, sandals would have failed crossing a pool because of Rav Ashi's ban on crossing water in sandals, so he had no permissible footwear for the trip. His absence was a halachic constraint, not a slight to the Exilarch.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara asks whether crossing a river or stream on Yom Kippur constitutes the prohibited bathing. The answer depends on intent and manner of crossi

display.whats: Sick person wearing shoes on YK; wooden shoes; danger exceptions
display.hint: A sick person may wear shoes on Yom Kippur for health reasons; the Gemara discusses wooden sandals and their status under the prohibition.

learning.summary: A sick person may wear shoes on Yom Kippur for health reasons; the Gemara discusses wooden sandals and their status under the prohibition.
learning.ahaMoment: Illness creates its own category on YK - even the shoe prohibition, which is about dignity and affliction, yields to health needs.
learning.memoryAnchor: Sick person's shoes: health needs override the affliction framework.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about sick person wearing shoes on yk; wooden shoes; danger except?

argumentFlow.label: Sitting on wet clay and cooling with fruit
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Yehuda bar Gerogarot forbids sitting on damp clay on Yom Kippur; Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi limits this to clay so wet it dampens what touches it, and Abaye requires it be wet enough to wet something else. Rav Yehuda permits cooling oneself with fruit, which is not considered bathing, as he himself did with a squash.

argumentFlow.label: Cooling vessels: full versus empty, silver versus ceramic
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabba cooled himself with a cold baby, Rava with a silver cup. Rav Pappa rules a full silver cup is forbidden but an empty one permitted; a ceramic cup is forbidden either way because water seeps through. Rav Ashi adds that even an empty silver cup is forbidden, since it may slip and spill.

argumentFlow.label: The pre-soaked, wrung-out cloth of Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Ze'eira bar Chama relays that Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi prepared a cloth before Yom Kippur by soaking and wringing it nearly dry, then wiped his face, hands, and feet with it the next day. For the eve of the Ninth of Av, a rabbinic fast, he soaked the cloth and passed it over his eyes.

argumentFlow.label: A dispute over which practice fit which fast
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabba bar Mari reported the practice with the days reversed. Rabbi Ya'akov challenged Rabbi Yirmeya bar Tachlifa that the wringing he described for Yom Kippur was really for the Ninth of Av, since Yom Kippur carries the same wringing prohibition as Shabbat and the cloth could not be wrung that day.

theological: Illness creates its own category on YK - even the shoe prohibition, which is abo
narrative: Sick person wearing shoes on YK; wooden shoes; danger except

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of sick person context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners think any cooling or wiping with moisture is simply bathing and therefore banned on Yom Kippur.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara permits cooling with fruit, an empty cup, or a pre-wrung cloth, because none provides the pleasure of washing; the controlling concerns are spilling water on the body and the separate prohibition of wringing, not contact with coolness as such.

visualizableElements.name: Sick person wearing shoes on YK; wooden shoes; dan

quizSeeds.q: Why is a full silver cup forbidden for cooling on Yom Kippur while an empty one is debated?
quizSeeds.a: A full cup risks spilling water onto the body, which approaches bathing. Rav Pappa permits an empty silver cup, but Rav Ashi forbids even that, since it may slip from the hand and spill. A ceramic cup is forbidden regardless because water seeps through its walls.

quizSeeds.q: What is the key distinction between the cloth practice for Yom Kippur and for the Ninth of Av?
quizSeeds.a: On Yom Kippur the cloth had to be soaked and wrung out before the fast, because wringing is forbidden on Yom Kippur just as on Shabbat. The Ninth of Av, being only rabbinic, allowed greater leniency with the cloth, which is why the two practices were not interchangeable.

sugyot.finalRuling: A sick person may wear shoes on Yom Kippur for health reasons; the Gemara discusses wooden sandals and their status under the prohibition.

display.whats: Rain water, river crossing, measuring bathing by amount
display.hint: Crossing rain puddles or rivers on YK raises questions about the bathing prohibition; the measurement of 'bathing' is discussed.

learning.summary: Crossing rain puddles or rivers on YK raises questions about the bathing prohibition; the measurement of 'bathing' is discussed.
learning.ahaMoment: Rain water vs. river: the Gemara is precise about what type of water contact triggers which prohibition.
learning.memoryAnchor: Rain puddles vs. rivers: the Gemara distinguishes types of water contact.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about rain water, river crossing, measuring bathing by amount?

argumentFlow.label: Must a scholarly Elder get the Nasi's permission to permit firstborns?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Menashya bar Tachalifa relays a question put to Rabbi Elazar ben Pedat: must an Elder who studies in the yeshiva obtain the Nasi's authorization to rule firstborn animals permitted by inspecting their blemishes, as others must, or is his standing sufficient on its own?

argumentFlow.label: The reason behind the permission requirement
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara explains, citing Rav Idi bar Avin, that the Nasi's authority to grant permission was instituted to honor his house. So the question is whether even a yeshiva Elder must seek it to honor the Nasi, or is exempt. Rav Tzadok testified he saw Rabbi Yosei ben Zimra, a yeshiva Elder, ask the prior Nasi for such permission.

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Abba reframes the incident as a halachic question
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Abba corrects the account: Rabbi Yosei ben Zimra was a priest, and his query was not about the Nasi's permission but a point of law, whether priests, suspected of blemishing firstborns after the Temple's destruction, are believed. The matter was resolved by Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel's view that one suspect about himself is still believed about others.

theological: Rain water vs. river: the Gemara is precise about what type of water contact tri
narrative: Rain water, river crossing, measuring bathing by amount

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of rain water context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners read the firstborn-permission passage as plainly about whether scholars must defer to the Nasi.
misconceptions.correction: Rabbi Abba shows the cited precedent was really a question of priestly credibility regarding blemishes, not of seeking the Nasi's authorization, so the supposed proof about a scholar needing permission does not actually establish that.

visualizableElements.name: Rain water, river crossing, measuring bathing by a

quizSeeds.q: Why was the Nasi's authority to grant permission to rule on firstborns established?
quizSeeds.a: To raise the stature of the Nasi's house. Because seeking his permission itself honors the Nasi, the question arises whether even a Torah scholar of standing must request it, or whether his own learning exempts him.

quizSeeds.q: How does Rabbi Abba reinterpret the testimony about Rabbi Yosei ben Zimra approaching the Nasi?
quizSeeds.a: He says it was not a request for permission at all but a halachic question. As a priest, suspected of blemishing firstborns after the Temple's destruction, Rabbi Yosei asked whether such priests are believed, resolved by the ruling that one suspect about himself is still believed about others.

sugyot.finalRuling: Crossing rain puddles or rivers on YK raises questions about the bathing prohibition; the measurement of 'bathing' is discussed.

display.whats: Unresolved question about water crossing and the shoe prohibition
display.hint: An unresolved question (teiku) about water crossing on YK and its relationship to the shoe and bathing prohibitions.

learning.summary: An unresolved question (teiku) about water crossing on YK and its relationship to the shoe and bathing prohibitions.
learning.ahaMoment: Some questions the Talmud leaves open - the unresolved teiku preserves intellectual honesty about the limits of halachic derivation.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about unresolved question about water crossing and the shoe prohib?

argumentFlow.label: May one wear cork sandals on Yom Kippur?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The same Sages raise a further question: what is the law regarding going out in sandals made of cork on Yom Kippur, given that the affliction forbids wearing a shoe. The question of whether such sandals count as a shoe is left for the discussion that continues onto the next page.

theological: Some questions the Talmud leaves open - the unresolved teiku preserves intellect
narrative: Unresolved question about water crossing and the shoe prohib

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners assume only conventional leather footwear is at issue in the shoe prohibition.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara probes borderline materials like cork precisely because the prohibition turns on whether an item functions as a comforting shoe, not on a fixed material, opening a real question about unconventional footwear.

visualizableElements.name: Unresolved question about water crossing and the s

quizSeeds.q: What is the core uncertainty in the question about cork sandals on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.a: Whether a cork sandal is legally a 'shoe' for the affliction of not wearing shoes. If it counts as a shoe it is forbidden; if it is too unlike a real shoe to provide that comfort, it may be permitted. The Gemara leaves this to resolve on the next page.

sugyot.finalRuling: An unresolved question (teiku) about water crossing on YK and its relationship to the shoe and bathing prohibitions.

rashiTranslations.he: מפכין. כמין הפך הן כשמגיעין למפתן ההיכל: מכאן רמז לנדה. מדלא קרי ליה ראוי לנדה עד שמתגבר כנחל שוטף שמגיע לפתח בית
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the Temple river aggadah - a trickle becomes a mighty torrent.

rashiTranslations.he: דוד רמז שצריכה לטבילת מים עמוקים שתשב בהן עד צוארה ואע"ג דלא רמיז קרא עד צוארה רמיז מיהא דניהוו עמוקים עומק הראוי וזה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the river's growth symbolizes the expanding influence of the Temple.

rashiTranslations.he: כראוי: התינח יום הכפורים לא גרסינן וה"ג שבת דאיכא מנעל מאי. מי שרי לעבור במים או דילמא חיישינן דילמא נפקי מכרעיה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: crossing a river on Yom Kippur - does this constitute prohibited bathing?

rashiTranslations.he: ואתי לאיתויינהו: ועברוה דרך מלבוש. בנעליהם עברו אותו: סנדל מאי. שאינו יכול להדקו ולקשרו יפה ברגלו כמו מנעל: איקלע
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the key factor is intent - crossing vs. bathing for pleasure.

rashiTranslations.he: להגרוניא. ושבת שם: לפירקא.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: a sick person may wear shoes on Yom Kippur for medical need.

rashiTranslations.he: לדרשא דריש גלותא: מדעתיה דריש
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: wooden shoes or clogs - their status under the sandal prohibition.

rashiTranslations.he: גלותא. שלא ישנאהו: איבעי לך
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: rain water that one walks through on Yom Kippur.

rashiTranslations.he: למירמא סנדל. שהוא רחב: על גבי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the measure needed for a water contact to constitute bathing.

rashiTranslations.he: טינא. שלחלוח הטיט הוי עונג
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 78a.

rashiTranslations.he: קרוב לרחיצה: מטפחת. מקיאה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 78a.

rashiTranslations.he: מים: מלא אסור. שמא ישפכו מים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 78a.

rashiTranslations.he: על בשרו: משחל שחיל. פולט מים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 78a.

rashiTranslations.he: שבולע טרישטריש"א בלע"ז: דמזדריב.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: מחליק ונשמט מידו וישפכו המים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: עליו: מטפחת. סודר כמין כלים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: נגובים ואני שמעתי דה"ג ערב יום

rashiTranslations.he: הכפורים מביאין לו מטפחת ומקנח
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: בה ידיו ולמחר פושטה ומעבירה על

rashiTranslations.he: עיניו והיא לחה קצת מקינוח הידים

rashiTranslations.he: דאתמול: איפכא אמרת לן. בתשעה

rashiTranslations.he: באב עושה אותן כמין כלים נגובים

rashiTranslations.he: ובערב יוה"כ לא אמרת עושה אותן

rashiTranslations.he: כמין כלים נגובים ואותיבנך סחיטה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: לשון ששמעתי הכי קאמר איפכא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: אמרת לן ערב תשעה באב מקנח בה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: ידיו ולמחר פושטה וכו' וערב יום
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: הכפורים שורה אותה במים ועושה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אותה כמין כלים נגובים ואותיבנך
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: סחיטה דכמין כלים נגובים עדיין יש
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: כדי לסחוט: שאלו את רבי אלעזר.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: גרסינן והוא רבי אלעזר בן פדת:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: זקן ויושב בישיבה וכו'. אמרינן
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בסנהדרין (דף ה.) שאין חכם מתיר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: את הבכור ביחיד לראות את מומו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אלא אם כן נטל רשות מן הנשיא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: פעם אחת והכא מיבעיא להו דאם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: זקן יושב בישיבת סנהדרין הוא מי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: צריך רשות' או לא והא דנקט לה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: הכא משום דבעי למימר ותו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: here it is so because the Gemara wishes to go on and ask further,

rashiTranslations.he: קמבעיא להו מהו לצאת בסנדל של
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: and they further inquired - what is the law of going out in a sandal of

rashiTranslations.he: שעם ביוה"כ: דבר זה. נטילת
rashiTranslations.en: cork on Yom Kippur. 'This matter' - taking

rashiTranslations.he: הרשות: להתגדר. לשון גדולה:
rashiTranslations.en: is a matter of discretion. 'Le-hitgader' - an expression of greatness.

rashiTranslations.he: למעלה מזקנו של זה. לפני זקינו של
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'above the beard of this one' - means before the elder of

rashiTranslations.he: נשיא זה שבדורנו: כהנים חשודין על
rashiTranslations.en: the nasi of our generation. 'Kohanim are suspect regarding blemishes of the firstborn'

rashiTranslations.he: מומי הבכור להתיר את האסור ואף
rashiTranslations.en: - to permit what is forbidden, and even

rashiTranslations.he: להטיל בו מום לפי שהבכור בזמן הזה
rashiTranslations.en: to inflict a blemish on it, since nowadays the firstborn animal

rashiTranslations.he: אין לו תקנה אלא אם כן יש בו מום
rashiTranslations.en: has no remedy unless it has a blemish,

rashiTranslations.he: ועל הכהן ליטפל בו משנתנוהו לו
rashiTranslations.en: and it falls to the kohen to tend it once it has been given to him

rashiTranslations.he: לשומרו ולזונו דרבי מאיר ודרבן
rashiTranslations.en: to guard and feed - this is the view of R. Meir and R. Shimon

rashiTranslations.he: שמעון במסכת בכורות (דף לה.): ותו
rashiTranslations.en: in tractate Bekhorot (35a). 'And they further',

rashiTranslations.he: קמבעיא להו. קמיה דרבי אלעזר:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: inquired before R. Elazar.

rashiTranslations.he: שעם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi begins the next comment ('she'am' - cork), opening the following passage.
